content: Just as the linguistic definition of reflexion frames it as both a passive optical phenomenon (light reflection) and an active deliberate cognitive practice (personal reflection), LLM self-reflection mechanisms can be redesigned to split functionality into two complementary modes: a passive 'reflection' mode that mirrors environmental feedback and task inconsistencies back to the core model, and an active 'reflexion' mode that deliberately interrogates past outputs to revise faulty reasoning. This dual functional split draws directly from the dual definition of the term to resolve a key limitation of current single-mode LLM self-reflection systems that struggle to balance spontaneous capture of inconsistencies with targeted error correction.
